summary: Dan Shapiro breaks down the real value of startup stock options, arguing that most employees dramatically overestimate what their equity is worth. He explains how liquidation preferences, participation rights, and dilution from funding rounds significantly erode the value of common shares compared to preferred investor shares. The post provides a practical heuristic formula for estimating option value that accounts for company failure risk, exit expectations, capital raised, and investor-friendly terms. Through a worked example, he shows that 0.1% of a company expecting a $250-500mm exit might only be worth $35k after adjustments. His motivation is transparency: he believes informed candidates will recognize genuinely good equity offers.
key_points: Startup employees typically take a ~33% pay cut, and the equity meant to compensate them is usually worth far less than presented
Preferred stock structures (liquidation preferences, participation rights, protective provisions) dramatically devalue common shares held by employees
The IRS rule of thumb valued common stock at one-tenth of preferred stock, meaning the headline 'company valuation' is misleading for option holders
Shapiro provides a concrete heuristic formula: take the low exit estimate halved, subtract total capital raised (doubled) times a penalty factor derived from preference terms and board composition
90% of startups fail, so if you don't deeply believe in the company, assume your options are worthless
money_quotes: A great startup with a dozen or so people will typically pay its employees about a third less than a big company.
The existence of preferred, 'investor' shares significantly devalues regular, common shares.
I don't want them misled with a number that's too big, or there's hell to pay when they realize they've been had.
90% of new companies fail, so if you don't, then you should assume your stock is worthless, and stop here. Also, you should go work elsewhere, because life's too short.
He's so good at closing candidates that before he does it, he takes on voluntary challenges. It's like he's working on an xbox achievement.
At most startups, the equity's worth a lot less than you might imagine.
I love startups. I love it when people get rich from startups. I want you to join a startup, and I want it to shower you with riches beyond your wildest dreams.
key_insight: Startup stock options are worth far less than headline valuations suggest once you account for liquidation preferences, dilution, and the base rate of startup failure, and employees should demand transparency to evaluate equity offers realistically.
